raw_text: S. Kyriakidis. " Georgics 4.559-566. The Vergilian Sphragis un Addendum di F. De Martino) in Kleos: Estemporaneo di stu e testi sulla fortuna dell'antico. Francesco De Martino, ed. 2002. 275-287; 288-289. Wiik examines the form and content of the excursus at Georgics 1.461-514, which is by design part of a didactic project relating to the cosmos. The catalogue of portents is analyzed from the perspective of the didactic genre. Kaster provides a general account of the emotion of invidia and applies some of its implications to a pivotal passage in the Georgics. Nappa's study of bilingual wordplay brings coolness suggestively into the foreground: "cold blood will have surrounded my consciousness ... set me down in the cool vale of Haemus." Elsewhere, Nappa argues that fire lies at the root of human failure in the steady process of labor; failure of success rests with human choice. Egan grapples convincingly with the interpretation and implications of quo muñere (520).

Beck-Chauvard studies the grieving mother of Euryalus ( Aen . 9.481-3) and Dido as Vergil's female community in grief, comparable to paternal sorrows of Mezentius and Evander. Boyd's revision of Pharr's monumental school text includes updates and additions to provide support for Advanced Placement classes. Clausen's study of decorum, allusion and ideology, is a greatly revised version of his Berkeley Sather Lecture (1987), entitled Vergil's Aeneid and the Tradition of Hellenistic Poetry. The La Fleur/McKay Upper Level student book (hardcover) includes Advanced Placement readings and meets requirements of the International Baccalaureate Latin syllabus. A substantial Teacher's Guide contains extensive discussion and teaching tips, literal translation of all selections, a bibliography and resource list. Galinsky's perceptive comments on drama in the epic are instructive, insightful and valid. Perkell's important article studies contradictions as a recurrent feature of Vergil's work and recognizes interpretive challenges faced by scholars and others. The text offers no resolution of the contradictions in Vergil's presentation of the Golden Age; they abide as Vergil's acknowledgment of a complex world replete with uncertainties. Putnam's article deals with ways to assess the epic and is both direct and familiar. 122 Alexander G. McKay AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS l-VI

raw_text: Shirley Werner, '"Frigid Indifference' or: 'Soaked Through and Through with Feeling'? Portrayal of Character in the Aeneid." APPROACHES 60-68. Chew's remarks on ignorance, uncertainty and inconsistency Aeneas' nature are helpful. Hughes examines the structure and char terization of Hector's return to Troy and his meeting with Androma (Iliad 6) to help shape our understanding of the relationship betwee Aeneas and Dido. Merriam sees the appearances of Ascanius as barometer of storms and turmoil and near disasters. Alden Smith imparts a "modern" touch to Dido's "performance" in the guise of a chanteuse, a British diva whose stanzas relate the loss of her "cowboy" in "My Lover's Gone" ["No Angel" album, Arista Records 1999]. Starr shows how experience with rhetoric and oratory enriches Vergil's presentation of Aeneas and his dilemma at Aeneid 4.279-285. The Bacchic associations of Amata, another tragic queen, as frenzied Bacchant, and Aeneas' dual nature, both Apolline and Dionysian, offer readers and critics important new avenues for reflection on the ambiguity and characterization of two memorable performers. 126 Alexander G. McKay
